title: The Email That Changed Everything
story:
There was this moment last year that reminded me why I do what I do. Someone I was helping—let's call her Mira—had been putting off a conversation with her manager for six months. Six months of feeling undervalued, stewing silently, getting more resentful every week. She'd drafted the email maybe twenty times but could never hit send.

When she showed me her draft, I understood why. It was three paragraphs of justification before she even got to the ask. Classic over-explaining—what we do when we don't feel entitled to want something.

So I asked her: 'What do you actually want to say? Like, if you had one sentence.'

She thought for a second and said: 'I want to be paid fairly for what I'm doing.'

Nine words. Clear. True. Not aggressive, not apologetic.

We rebuilt the email around that. Lead with value. Specific accomplishments. Clear ask. End with invitation.

She sent it. And within an hour—an hour—her manager replied asking to schedule a conversation. A week later, she had a 15% raise.

But here's the part that really got me. She messaged me after and said: 'The raise is great. But what really changed is that I feel like I can advocate for myself now. Like I have permission to ask for things.'

That's the thing about communication coaching. It's not really about the words. It's about helping people believe they're allowed to ask for what they need. Once they believe that, the words come naturally.
